g.title: Completed Soil Sensor Circuit Test
g.desc: This diagram displays a completed circuit on a rectangular breadboard, with a soil sensor connected for testing. On the breadboard, locate a flat, green rectangular board (like a small computer) plugged into the holes on the left side. In the center, a black rectangular chip with many thin legs is plugged in, straddling the breadboard's central groove. Various small, cylindrical resistors with wire leads and colorful, flexible jumper wires connect these components across the breadboard. Now, find the soil sensor: it's a flat, 5mm thick wooden stick, with a pointed bottom and two parallel metal strips running along one face. A red plastic-covered alligator clip is attached from a resistor connected on the breadboard to one of the metal strips on the wooden soil sensor. A black plastic-covered alligator clip connects from another resistor on the breadboard to the second metal strip of the soil sensor. The pointed end of the wooden soil sensor is shown partially submerged in water within a clear, round cup, indicating it's ready for moisture measurement.

g.title: Kit Inventory: Verify All Parts
g.desc: This is a list of all the materials included in your kit. Before you start building, carefully feel each item to identify it and count the quantity to ensure you have everything listed. You should find: * **1 Plastic Stake:** A single, rigid plastic piece, likely slender and possibly with a pointed end or a specific shape for insertion. * **1 Copper Tape:** A roll or strip of tape that feels smooth and distinctly metallic, with one side possibly sticky. * **1 Sticker:** A thin, flat piece of material, often paper or plastic, with an adhesive (sticky) backing. * **2 Alligator Clips:** Two small, metal clips with spring-loaded jaws that open and close. They often have plastic covers or grips on their handles. * **1 Bar Graph LED:** One rectangular electronic component that feels like a small block. It will likely have a row of tiny bumps (the LEDs) on one face and several metal pins extending from one edge. * **2 1M ohm Resistors:** Two tiny, cylindrical electronic components, each with a thin wire lead sticking out from both ends. They often have small colored bands on their body. * **10 330 ohm Resistors:** Ten more tiny, cylindrical electronic components, similar in feel to the 1M ohm resistors, also with wire leads. Note the larger quantity for this type. * **2 Aluminum Square Tape:** Two pieces or strips of tape that feel like thin, flexible metal, possibly cut into squares or rectangular strips, with one side that may be sticky.

g.title: Gather and Organize Your Kit Parts
g.desc: This blueprint shows various kit components to help you organize your supplies. Locate the single plastic stake, a long, flat, rigid plastic piece with one blunt end and one pointed end. Next, find the single sticker, a thin, flexible rectangle similar in shape to the plastic stake, with one pointed end and a sticky back. Identify the roll of copper tape, a thin, metallic-feeling strip wound into a roll. Find the single Bar Graph LED, a small, flat, rectangular electronic component, about an inch long, with a row of tiny, subtle bumps or divots on its top surface and small metal pins extending from its edges. Discover the two pieces of aluminum square tape, thin, flexible square sheets that feel like heavy-duty aluminum foil with one sticky side. You will also find twelve tiny, cylindrical electronic resistors, about the size of a grain of rice, each with thin wire leads extending from both ends; these are separated into a bag with two '1 Mega-ohm' resistors and another bag with ten '330 Ohm' resistors. Finally, gather the two alligator clip wires, each a flexible wire about 1 foot long, with a small metal clip on both ends that you can squeeze open.

g.title: Locate Plastic Stake Component
g.desc: Find the single plastic piece. It will feel different from the wooden parts, likely smoother and potentially more flexible. Tactilely, it is a long, flat shape. One end is rounded like a soft, curved corner. The opposite end narrows to a triangular point, similar to an arrow tip or a sharpened pencil.

g.title: One Flexible Copper Tape Piece
g.desc: This component is a flexible, metallic copper tape strip. To identify it, feel for a very thin, smooth, and flexible strip of material. It measures approximately 6 inches in length and 0.25 inches in width. One side may have a subtle sticky texture if it has an adhesive backing.

g.title: Bar Graph LED Module
g.desc: Locate the Bar Graph LED module. This is a small, flat, rectangular plastic electronic component, approximately one inch long. On one of its flat surfaces, you will feel a slightly raised, smooth strip containing a row of ten small, individual rectangular segments. These are the LEDs. Along one of the shorter edges, you will find a row of thin, metal pins sticking out, which are used for connections.

g.title: Two Aluminum Square Tapes
g.desc: Locate two pieces of aluminum tape. Each piece is a thin, flexible square, measuring approximately one inch on each side. One surface will feel smooth and cool like metal foil, while the other side may feel slightly sticky or have a paper backing.

g.title: Locate the Soil Sensor Label Sticker
g.desc: This component is a single vinyl sticker, which feels thin, flexible, and smooth to the touch, unlike the rigid 5mm thick plywood parts. Tactilely, identify its distinct elongated shape: one end is flat or blunt, while the opposite end tapers sharply to a point, like the tip of an arrow. This unique pointed end helps you orient the sticker. It serves as a label for the soil sensor.

g.title: Two 1M Ohm Resistors
g.desc: Locate two small, cylindrical electronic components. Each resistor has a rigid body, about the size of a small bead or a grain of rice. From each end of this body, a thin, flexible wire extends. There is no specific orientation needed for these components as they are symmetrical.

g.title: 333 Ohm Resistors (x10)
g.desc: Locate ten small, smooth, and rigid cylindrical components. Each resistor is about the size of a small bead or a cooked grain of rice. You will feel a thin, flexible metal wire extending from both ends of the cylinder. These wires are for connecting the resistor to other parts.

g.title: Alligator Clip Wires
g.desc: Find two flexible cables. Each cable has a metal alligator clip on both ends. To identify the clips, squeeze them to feel them open and close, and feel for the small 'teeth' on the inside that grip things. The clips also have smooth plastic covers where you hold them. One entire cable, including the plastic parts of its clips, is red. The other entire cable, including its clips' plastic parts, is black. Both cables are smooth, flexible, and each is about one foot long.

g.title: Position Copper Tape on Stake
g.desc: Identify the flat, rigid plastic stake; it has a blunt, straight edge at one end and tapers to a point at the other. Lay the stake flat, with the blunt end facing you. Take one strip of copper tape – it's thin and sticky on one side. Carefully peel the backing. Stick the tape onto the stake, running it lengthwise from near the blunt end towards the pointed end. Ensure you leave a small, bare plastic gap at the blunt end of the stake. Now, take the second strip of copper tape and peel its backing. Stick this strip parallel to the first, leaving a similar small, bare plastic gap between the two strips. Both tape strips should also end before the pointed tip of the stake, leaving another small, bare plastic gap at that end.

g.title: Place Foil Tape Evenly on Top
g.desc: Find the boat-shaped plywood piece; it is 5mm thick, with a rounded end and a tapered, pointed end. This image shows the top surface of this piece. Now, find two long, thin strips of foil tape. The instructions show you should carefully place these two strips of tape onto the top surface of the boat piece. Position them so they run parallel to each other, lengthwise along the boat. Start the tape near the rounded end and extend it towards the pointed end, stopping before it reaches the very tip. Use your fingers to feel that the distance between the two tape strips is even, and that each strip is an equal distance from the outer edges of the boat, as indicated by the red spacing arrows.

g.title: Apply Sticker to Plastic Stake
g.desc: This step involves applying a rectangular sticker to the plastic soil sensor stake. First, locate the plastic stake; it's a long, flat piece, thicker than a ruler, with one end coming to a blunt point, like a dull pencil tip. Lay the stake flat so its widest, smoothest surface faces up. Next, find the rectangular sticker. Gently peel a small section of the paper backing from one end of the sticker to reveal its sticky side. Align this sticky end of the sticker onto one end of the plastic stake's wide surface, making sure it's centered. Slowly press the sticker down onto the stake, gradually peeling off the rest of the paper backing as you go. Use your fingers to smooth the sticker from the center outwards to prevent any air bubbles or wrinkles.

g.title: Connect Alligator Clips to Copper Tape
g.desc: For this step, you will need the flat, wooden 'soil sensor' stick and two small 'alligator clips' (one red, one black). First, identify the soil sensor. It's a flat piece of wood, about 5mm thick, shaped like a long stick with a pointed end at the bottom. Along its two long, flat sides, you will feel smooth, metallic strips of copper tape running down. The alligator clips are small, spring-loaded clips with 'teeth' that you can open by squeezing the back on the ends of the wires. One clip has a red wire and the other has a black wire. Take the red alligator clip, squeeze it open, and clip it firmly onto one of the copper tape strips on the soil sensor, near the flat, non-pointed end. Then, take the black alligator clip, squeeze it open, and clip it firmly onto the *other* copper tape strip on the soil sensor, also near the top. Ensure both clips are making good contact with their respective copper tape strips. The image also shows the sensor placed upright in a cup of water, which is for testing and calibration, but your primary task for this step is to connect the clips to the sensor.

g.title: Assembly Complete: Sensor Circuit
g.desc: This image signifies the completion of your assembly. Tactilely, you should have a flat, rectangular white plastic breadboard. A smaller, rigid green circuit board is mounted on it, with its metal pins inserted into the breadboard holes. Adjacent to it, a small, black rectangular electronic module is also inserted into the breadboard. Numerous colored jumper wires connect from the green board to the black module. You should also have a light-brown, flat soil moisture sensor. Identify its pointed end, which has two parallel metal probes, and its wider end, which contains a small raised circuit component. Two wires with alligator clips, one red and one black, are connected from the breadboard to the sensor. The red alligator clip is attached to one of the metal connection points on the wider end of the sensor, and the black alligator clip to the other. The opposite ends of these red and black wires are plugged into specific holes on the breadboard, near the green board. The pointed end of the soil moisture sensor is shown placed inside a rigid, cylindrical plastic cup, with its metal probes submerged, ready for testing.
